metadata.title: Why Your Beautiful Resume Is Invisible
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 06. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/13-resumes-that-survive-the-filter.md (the ATS-parser read mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most; a résumé page under a horizontal read-head as the hero object
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty navy = text that extracts clean / survives the parser, crimson = scrambled / vanished / lost, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty navy #3D5A80 = extracts clean / survives; crimson #BF3339 = scrambled / vanished / lost to the parser; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO ATS vendor comparisons (no Greenhouse/Lever/etc.), NO OCR tangent (the film says name-in-image is invisible and moves on — never explains OCR), NO résumé-content/keyword advice, NO 82%-stat deep-dive (the stat appears in ONE beat, B09, as context — never dissected), NO Markdown/Playwright pipeline walkthrough (resumes:pdf never shown). The paste test appears ONLY as the ~5-second closing payoff (B11). One mechanism: a parser extracts embedded text in document order, so a designed layout scrambles before any human opens it. Verified from ch.13: document-order extraction; two-column interleave; name-in-graphic absent (parser reads embedded text, cannot OCR); skill bars produce no text; ~82% of companies screen with software by 2025 and ~1 in 5 auto-reject with no human review (chapter footnote flags 82% [verify] vs primary source).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The first reader of your résumé can't see. And the best-looking document you've ever made is the one it can't read.

card.copy: Why your beautiful résumé is invisible
card.sub: the first reader is a parser

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] the beautiful invisible résumé

beats.narration_text: Here it is. A weekend's work. Two columns, a sidebar of skill bars, your name set large in a header graphic. Beautiful.
beats.scene_description: a gorgeous two-column résumé pinned to the newsprint ground — header banner with a stylized name, a skills sidebar of proficiency bars, section icons; slow push-in across the design
beats.image_prompt: printed screenshot of a beautifully designed two-column résumé, header banner with a large stylized name, a sidebar of labeled skill proficiency bars, small section icons, elegant typography, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, desaturated flat print reproduction, editorial collage

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [PHOTO] the beautiful two-column résumé — push-in

beats.narration_text: The parser doesn't care that it's beautiful. It cares about one thing: can it pull structured text out of the page?

document.attribution: — the only question the parser asks

beats.scene_description: the parser's whole job as a large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'extract' as it is spoken

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [QUOTE] can I extract the text?

beats.narration_text: Because the first reader isn't a person. It's a parser — and it reads your whole page as one strip of text, top to bottom.

production_viz.label: the read-head pulls text into a strip
production_viz.mechanic: a résumé page on the left; a navy horizontal read-head sweeps top to bottom; as it passes, lines stream out into a single vertical navy strip on the right — the scan move
production_viz.note: the hero object — established clean here, broken in B05-B08

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] read-head sweeps, text becomes one strip

viz.label: the page read as one linear strip

beats.narration_text: Two columns? It reads straight across, left to right. Line one of the left column lands beside line one of the right. The order becomes nonsense.

production_viz.label: columns interleave into nonsense
production_viz.mechanic: a two-column page; the read-head sweeps across the full width; the output strip alternates L1, R1, L2, R2 — the interleaved lines turn crimson to mark the scramble
production_viz.note: break mode 1 — document-order left-to-right across both columns

beats.narration_text: Your name, set inside a header image? It can't read pictures — only embedded text. So your name comes through simply absent.
beats.scene_description: the header banner with the stylized name; the read-head passes over it; the strip position where the name should be stays blank; a crimson label 'name: absent'

production_viz.label: name in a graphic → absent
production_viz.mechanic: the header name-graphic sits at the top; read-head passes; the corresponding strip slot is an empty crimson-outlined blank; label 'name: absent'
production_viz.note: break mode 2 — parser reads embedded text, not images. NO OCR tangent.

beats.new_visual_element: [COMPOSITE] name-in-graphic → a blank in the strip

beats.narration_text: Those elegant skill bars are graphics too. They produce no text at all — a blank gap where your skills should be.

production_viz.label: skill bars → no text
production_viz.mechanic: the sidebar skill bars; read-head passes; the strip slot for skills is an empty crimson gap; label 'skills: (nothing)'
production_viz.note: break mode 3 — graphic elements produce no extractable text

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] skill bars → a gap in the strip

beats.narration_text: Now the parser can't find one clean job title. Name missing, dates drifting, skills gone. It scores you unqualified for everything.
beats.scene_description: the full scrambled strip; crimson tags mark 'name: —', 'dates: drifting', 'skills: —'; a crimson 'UNQUALIFIED' stamp lands

production_viz.label: no title found → unqualified
production_viz.mechanic: the scrambled strip persists; three crimson field tags (name/dates/skills broken); a terracotta-crimson 'UNQUALIFIED' stamp slaps on at a slight angle
production_viz.note: the payoff of the three break modes

beats.narration_text: And it happens before a human ever opens it. Around eight in ten companies screen this way, and one in five applications is cut with no human review.

production_viz.label: the fork before the human
production_viz.mechanic: 'Submit' splits into two paths: a navy path 'parses → human → interview' and a crimson path 'scrambled → auto-reject → no human'; a serif label marks the fork 'before any human opens it'
production_viz.note: Fig 13.1. Stat appears once here — ~82% screen with software, ~1 in 5 auto-rejected. No deep-dive.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the fork — rejected before a human looks

viz.label: two paths from Submit; the fork is before the human

beats.narration_text: The fix is boring, and it works. One column, real text, plain headings — and the same content comes through clean, in order, ready for a human.
beats.scene_description: a single-column page; the read-head sweeps; the output strip is clean ordered navy lines (name, title, dates, skills) with navy check tags; label 'one column · real text'

production_viz.label: single column → clean strip
production_viz.mechanic: single-column page; read-head sweeps; strip fills with ordered navy lines; small navy check tags for name/title/dates; label 'one column · real text'
production_viz.note: the safe structures — single column, real text, plain headings. No keyword/content advice.

beats.narration_text: Want to see what the parser sees? Select all, copy, paste. Five seconds.

card.copy: Select all, copy, paste — that's what the parser sees.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — the 5-second paste test
